The surgeon identifies the gastrocolic loop of Henle, formed by the right gastrocolic vein, accessory glycolic vein, and ASPDV. The accessory glycolic vein is dissected and taken down, followed by clipping the gastrocolic trunk as it enters the superior mesenteric vein (SMV).
The surgeon assesses the space available to divide the gastrocolic trunk. If insufficient space is present, individual arteries or veins are clipped to facilitate their removal.
The surgeon takes down the right gastroepiploic vein and divides the gastrocolic vein, completing the dissection of these vascular structures.
The surgeon tracks the anatomical structures to visualize the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and creates a partial tunnel behind the neck of the pancreas.
Then we move on to caucarization.
The assistant vigorously pulls the second part of the duodenum, allowing the surgeon to enter the proper anatomical planes for further dissection.
Sometimes it may be necessary to bring down the hepatic flexure, but sometimes it may not be necessary.
The surgeon exposes the inferior vena cava (IVC) and proceeds to detach the attachments of the third part of the duodenum, following the coccurase of the second part.

The surgeon completes an extended Kocherization, releasing the DJ flexure. In the laparoscopic approach, the uncinate process is addressed first. The DJ flexure is fully immobilized, and the jejunum is divided 15 centimeters away from the flexure using a 60 mm stapler. The mesentery is dissected down to the level of the DJ flexure and repositioned into the supracolic compartment.

The surgeon reflects the duodenum in a caudal to cranial direction towards the head, revealing the inferior pancreaticoduodenal vessels branching from the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and vein (SMV). After clipping and cutting the inferior pancreaticoduodenal vein, the SMA becomes visible, and slow dissection is performed close to the vessel.

The surgeon divides the inferior pancreaticoduodenal veins, which may vary in number. Upon reflecting the superior mesenteric vein (SMV), the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) becomes visible. The inferior pancreaticoduodenal artery is seen arising from the SMA, and the inferior pancreaticoduodenal vessels are observed draining into the SMV.
The surgeon clips and dissects another pancreaticoduodenal vessel, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.

The advantage of dividing them before transecting pancreas is the bleeding will be very less. So almost most of the pancreas duodenal vessels were taken down before any important step so that the bleeding will be very less.
The surgeon lifts the falciform ligament using transabdominal sutures, elevating it away from the liver surface.

The surgeon transects the stomach, narrowing down the right gastroepiploic arcade. Both the artery and vein are clipped before performing a pylorus-resecting pancreaticoduodenectomy, proximal to the pylorus.

The surgeon takes down the right gastric artery from the right hepatic or common hepatic artery. The lesser sac is opened and released proximally to mobilize the stomach for anastomosis. The stomach is then transected using a golden 60 mm stapler, completing two important transections: the jejunum and the stomach.

The surgeon performs a lymphadenectomy on the lymph node located on the common hepatic artery. The left hand rolls down the superior border of the pancreas, creating a plane between the node and the pancreas. A vessel sealing device is used to expose the common hepatic artery, which branches into the proper hepatic artery above and the gastroduodenal artery below.

The surgeon performs lymphadenectomy along the hepatoduodenal ligament (HDL), completing the removal of lymph nodes in this area. The gastroduodenal artery is dissected and temporarily clamped to assess pulsations, then double clipped, leaving a visible stump.

The surgeon leaves a one-centimeter stump after dividing the gastroduodenal artery (GDA) and reflects the tissue to perform lymphadenectomy behind it. The portal vein is exposed following the division of the GDA, with the tunnel completed for superior mesenteric vein (SMV) and portal vein (PV) access. Small attachments are removed, revealing the splenic vein branching into the portal vein.
The surgeon identifies an avascular plane and loops the neck of the pancreas using umbilical tape, tying it proximally with the portal vein visible above.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the uncinate process from the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), visibly separating these structures before proceeding to transect the neck of the pancreas.

The surgeon uses monopolar cautery to transect the neck of the pancreas after the complete division of the pancreaticoduodenal artery. Bleeding is anticipated in two areas: the upper and lower poles of the pancreas.

The surgeon exposes the pancreatic duct and sharply divides it without using an energy source. The cephalic part of the pancreas is also divided, addressing a bleeding point. The common bile duct is dissected from the portal vein, and the postoperative pancreaticoduodenal vein is divided. Lymph nodes behind the hepatic ductal ligament are removed, revealing the lower border of the liver. The gallbladder is decompressed as the final step.

The surgeon dissects the cystic duct and artery, taking down the cystic artery. The right hepatic artery is visible moving anterior to the common bile duct (CBD), aiding in traction for exposure. The cystic duct is divided due to its high entry and parallel course with the CBD. Following lymphadenectomy, the CBD is divided distal to the right hepatic artery to facilitate anastomosis.

The surgical field reveals the bed after pancreaticoduodenectomy, with visible structures including the portal vein, splenic vein, and superior mesenteric vein (SMV). The SMA region is also exposed, with cospes placed on the common bile duct (CBD).

The surgeon identifies the inferior vena cava (IVC), left renal vein, and superior mesenteric artery (SMA). The jejunum is brought through the mesocolic window to the right of the middle colic artery. A pancreaticojejunostomy is performed using the modified Blunger technique due to the large pancreatic duct and firm pancreas.

The surgeon places two through-and-through sutures on the atrophic pancreas using 2-0 Prolene, one cephalad and one caudal to the duct. A thick seromuscular bite is taken from the jejunum and then back through the pancreas from posterior to anterior, occluding vessels and minor ducts. The jejunum's seromuscular layer snugly fits into the posterior part of the pancreas. After completing ductomucosa sutures with 4-0 Prolene PDS, the first bite is taken at the eleven o'clock position.

The surgeon takes a second suture bite at the six o'clock or seven o'clock position on the pancreatic duct, creating a gap between the first and second bites. After ligating, the sutures are retracted to close the gap, with five sutures placed in the posterior wall and five in the anterior wall of the duct.

The surgeon completes the posterior layer of interrupted sutures for the ductal mucosa during the pancreaticojejunostomy, noting the slightly atrophic appearance of the pancreas. The anterior layer suturing begins from the eleven to twelve o'clock position, with four to five sutures being placed. An instrument is introduced into the ducts to prevent suturing through the posterior layer of the duct.

The surgeon places the last two sutures under direct vision for the ductomucosa anastomosis, ensuring there is no gap. The assistant holds the sutures in position before tying them. Once tied, the previous suture is also ligated, completing the ductomucosa anastomosis. Additional superficial sutures may be taken if needed to ensure completeness.
The surgeon carefully places the last 4-0 PDS suture, ensuring shallow bites to avoid incorporating the posterior wall of the main pancreatic duct (MPD) into the suture.

The surgeon takes a proline suture through the anterior wall of the jejunum, rolling it over the anterior part of the pancreas, similar to the posterior placement. Capsular to seromuscular bites are taken on the anterior wall of the jejunum at the duct site, ensuring a snug fit over the pancreas. A through-and-through bite is taken caudally, mirroring the previous keflak bite, to cover the transected pancreatic surface. The jejunum is positioned to cover the pancreas completely, completing the modified Blumgart technique for pancreaticojejunostomy. The procedure then transitions to hepaticojejunostomy, with the epigastric port used to retract the left liver lobe, and the first suture placed in the right corner.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 PDS V-Lock suture to perform a continuous suturing technique on the anterior and posterior walls of the wide duct and jejunum. Approximately eight to ten bites are taken due to the duct's wide nature. The initial sutures are challenging, but once placed, they bring the duct and jejunum together. Corner sutures are taken individually, with the left corner suture brought outside the jejunum using the left hand.

The surgeon completes the posterior layer of sutures using a V-lock suture, carefully angling with the left hand to avoid the posterior wall of the jejunum or CBD. The anterior layer is then sutured with eight to ten bites between the CBD and jejunum, ensuring precise retraction and visualization. A small clip is applied for safety, and the absence of bile leak is confirmed.

The gallbladder is removed, and surgical drains are placed near the hepatico-jejunostomy and pancreatic jejunostomy sites. A loop of jejunum is selected and tied to the stomach, with a small incision made at the umbilicus for gastrojejunostomy (GJ) and feeding jejunostomy (FJ).
